topic: FL Studio MIDI import partitioning and isolated automation CC export
context: The musical generator remains unchanged, but final MIDI export should be partitioned for stable manual FL Studio import and explicit automation curve mapping.
observation: Use at most 30 FL import tracks or instrument targets per instrument MIDI file by conservative user-reported policy. Treat automation MIDI as isolated CC curve transport only, not as native FL automation creation or parameter binding.

result.instrument_export_policy: Partition instrument MIDI exports into multiple Type 1 files when more than 30 FL import tracks/instrument targets are needed.
result.automation_export_policy: Prefer one automation target per MIDI-CC file with one CC number, one MIDI channel, one target parameter, length in bars/BPM, and a mapping-file entry.

source_method: No live FL Studio import smoke performed
known_pitfalls: Do not interpret the 30-track export policy as a change to Standard MIDI channel range.
Do not claim MIDI-CC import creates or binds FL automation clips.
Avoid packing many unrelated CCs into one automation MIDI file for manual import.
MIDI CC resolution is coarse for precise EQ, fine gain, mastering, and exact sidechain curves.
reproduction_steps: Generate a plan with more than 30 instrument/import tracks.
Export instrument MIDI in multiple Type 1 files, each with track_count <= 30.
Export one automation target per MIDI-CC file.
Create a JSON/YAML mapping file from each automation MIDI file to the manual FL automation clip or target parameter.
open_questions: The exact FL Studio build-specific failure threshold for large MIDI imports has not been measured.
Whether a multi-track automation MIDI file remains practical depends on the manual import workflow and target project complexity.
next_recommended_action: When FL Studio is available, run a manual import smoke comparing 30-track and 31-track instrument MIDI files and document the build, import settings, and result.
